Almost all major government agencies have their own public-relations staff, and in many cases it is a stand-alone department with a public relations or media director and several support staff. Government agency PR departments are responsible for producing news releases, holding press conferences, and generally promoting activities of the agency, such as tourism or encouraging new businesses to move into the area.
Government agencies are constantly buying supplies, equipment and other property and selling off old equipment and property. The departments tasked with disposing of this government property often hire individuals with a background in marketing. Their job is to assist the agency in coming up with creative ways to sell or otherwise dispose of obsolete government property.
Most government agencies have to follow complicated regulations for purchasing supplies and equipment. In many cases, agencies can only buy from certain suppliers or can only buy goods within specific price ranges. Agencies often hire purchasing agents with marketing backgrounds to

a variety of capacities, each with their respective main responsibilities.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics reports that marketers account for only 15 percent of the total work force in the U.S. But 10 percent are in non-traditional industries like software or cloud computing, related services and support.

Ms. Rinaldi comes to the Middletown Township Public Library after having served as the Library Director of the Margaret E. Heggen Free Public Library in Washington Township, New Jersey. During her tenure in Washington Township, Ms. Rinaldi managed 26 employees and a $2.5 million annual budget. Ms. Rinaldi has also served as the Director of the Brielle Public Library and as a Senior Librarian at the Ocean County Library, Long Beach Island Branch in Surf City, New Jersey.
Ms. Rinaldi holds a Masters in Library and Information Science from Texas Women’s College and has extensive experience in library administration, budgeting and project management. Ms. Rinaldi is credited with outsourcing material cataloging and processing at the Margaret E. Heggan Free Public Library, reducing annual operating costs by $150,000.00. Ms. Rinaldi also managed a successful $2 million library building project.

She also is a strong communicator, possessing a positive, congenial presence that encourages students and patrons to take an active role in shaping the Library’s environment.

things that cause cancer list”, grouping them alongside tobacco, asbestos and diesel fumes, for which there is “sufficient evidence” of cancer links.
Along the years, there have been many studies that linked processed and red meats with cancer (especially bowel cancer), but there were always some doubts floating about. Now, the 22-member panel analyzed animal experiments, studies of human diet and health, and cell mechanisms that could lead from these meats to cancer.
“These findings further support current public health recommendations to limit intake of meat,” Dr. Christopher Wild, who directs IARC, said in a statement.
The decision wasn’t unanimous though, and a “probably” still crept in to the report. Many studies show the links, both in populations of people and in tests that show how eating these foods can cause cancer, the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) said in its report, released in the Lancet medical journal.

priority list”—the so-called "List of Significant Risk Factors for Cancer of the Oral cavity" published today. The listing is divided into five categories, but the agency calls out fat and sugar (page 10), the source of preservatives (page 29), the consumption of salt (page 55), and as part of one’s diet, consumption of processed meats and sugary foods (page 65).
As far as raising awareness, the IARC recommends that companies market the health risk of processed meats, but advises against over-investing in marketing campaigns as such “can be harmful for the animal industries.”
The report didn’t explicitly suggest why, but this has something to do with chemicals found in processed meats that protect them from radiation, but also limit cancer-causing UV rays—the cancer-causing “UVR” nuclear radionuclides such as Cs-137. It’s these uranium-containing compounds

QVC, which competes with ShopNBC and the HSN home shopping network, has not wanted to get caught up in the frenzy and has always treated it as just another day for its sales pitches, said Doug Rose, the network’s vice president of programming and marketing.
“This year, and we’ve been planning on this for many months, we really expect to join the fray with the other retailers,” Rose said.

bout of diarrhea, and some additional home care tips to consider.
Anyone who is experiencing persistent diarrhea should see a doctor, as a person may become dehydrated over time.
Diarrhea is a bowel movement that is more liquid than solid or has a loose texture. It is a common problem and may occur a couple of times each year. Diarrhea will usually last no more than 3 days.
Soft, bland foods, such as toast, will help to absorb excess water and ease the symptoms of diarrhea.
People with chronic digestive conditions, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) or Crohn's disease, may experience diarrhea more regularly.
A person's diet is crucial if they are experiencing diarrhea. Some foods might help relieve symptoms of diarrhea while eating other foods can make diarrhea worse.
While recovering from diarrhea, a person should eat bland, simple foods that are easy to digest and will help absorb some water from the stool.
These foods may be especially helpful

Nevada's effective monopoly on sports betting ended last spring, when the Supreme Court ruled the ban should be lifted. Casinos in seven other states — Rhode Island, Delaware, Mississippi, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, West Virginia and New Mexico — now accept wagers on sports. The list is expected to grow by next year with numerous states considering bills.
And there's already a hitch that could slow bets: NASCAR this season has decided for the first time to strip drivers of victories if their cars are deemed illegal. There will be a roughly 90-minute inspection after the race and the winner will not be deemed official until the process is completed. Will fans really want to linger around the pay window another couple of hours just to cash in a ticket?
"The state's risk manager will delay any payout until NASCAR releases the

But teams obviously also make their own luck when signing up for fantasy sports. Gaughan said Fox gave the car crews information for the teams so that NASCAR can maximize their odds.
The learning curve can be steeper, but time is what Gaughan prefers.
"It's short term," Gaughan said. "But you get better at it, especially before the races."

The most optimistic is a first round selection. Most likely, though, the call could come sometime during the second or third rounds.
Baseball America has projected Deardorff, who graduated from South Lake High School Friday, as the 54th overall pick, which would make him a second round choice.
Deardorff, though, isn't getting into guessing games about where, or even if, he is going to be taken.
"I have no idea," he said, about where he would be drafted. "People have said a lot of things, but I don't want to say anything in case it doesn't happen."
South Lake baseball coach Mike Crawford, though, thinks Deardorff will reach his long-time goal.
"His goal in life is to play pro ball," Crawford said. "The team that gets him will be extremely fortunate. They will be getting a kid with a great work ethic and a love of the game.
"

The more optimistic hope is that a decision - either he goes No. 100 or 114 in today's selections - comes long before the 7 p.m. CT start to the draft, scheduled to be broadcast nationally on ESPN.
Only eight high school players were drafted this last year - Deardorff was among them. This year, 27 players were chosen - three from Lake Charles - by a total of 20 teams. When Jaycee Horn of Oregon State and Richard Pitino of Florida State drafted Deardorff in 2016, it was believed the high school class of 2019 would have been easy prey for team negotiators as well.
The only school to miss out this year is LSU, where defensive back Victor Williams is scheduled to be selected in the fourth round. Last year, LSU didn't take any players from Louisiana and couldn't land a player because the draft was the No. 13 slot. But LSU knows this is another year in which it has to attract top-flight talent.

The same agency said in November that in the last fiscal year the UAE became the largest foreign purchaser of US defence equipment with sales of $7.9bn, ahead of Afghanistan ($5.4bn), Saudi Arabia ($3.3bn) and Taiwan ($3.2bn).
The spending included orders for munitions for the UAE’s F-16 fighter jets as well as a new Patriot defensive missile system and a fleet of corvettes for the navy.
The UAE purchases have been fuelled by a quadrupling of oil prices in the past decade and by continuing regional tensions, including those involving Iran, the US and Israel over the Islamic republic�

Sikorsky S70 Global Express helicopters and four HKRA9 SAM missiles over the past six months, it said in a statement on Friday.
The UAE's year 2017 was notable for the UAE signing its first US defence and security contract, running for $400 million and reportedly including around US$190 million worth of weapons, software and support.
This was followed by an award worth around US$210 million on the delivery of 23 advanced Boeing AH-64 Apache helicopters and two Russian-made Su-27 jets worth an estimated US$410 million.
The contracts led to more than 1,000 Emirati workers, most from engineering, becoming employees of the US firms and include 22 instances where the two companies used Emirati training facilities in the country, the Pentagon-run Information Task Force said.

individual states, he touted wins in Republican-leaning Georgia and Ohio, resisting calls from many in his party to keep the pressure on states and congressional Republicans in recent months.
Highlighting the logjams ahead for his health care legislation, Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid of Nevada announced he will bring legislation for a vote in the chamber as soon as next week. Linton's TV credits
Sometimes Linton is at the opposite end of her style, in others between. In "Airplane!" "The Da Vinci Code," "Kids," "Big Love" -- she's the regular looking guy. He said: "We are a First World economy, but I don't think we can say that we are a First World society.
"We need to think about how to not just do good for ourselves, but also for future generations.
"We need to think about what kind of society we aspire to be."
He was speaking yesterday at the launch of his book, Can Singapore Fall? Making The Future For Singapore, which is a compilation of three lectures he delivered between September and November last year.
Mr Lim said that while thinking about the lectures, he came to the conclusion that to succeed and avoid "social decay", Singaporeans needed to think about the kind of society they aspired to be.

’s Day 2007, Lynn Blake was embarking on a new job in Vail Village when she had a rude awakening. The 27-year-old suffered sudden cardiac arrest. Fortunately for her, someone trained in CPR stepped forward and kept her heart beating until emergency personnel arrived with a defibrillator. If someone dropped in front of you unconscious, with no notable pulse, would you know what to do? Some 325,000 people die every year of sudden cardiac arrest (SCA) in the United States. And, contrary to popular belief, it strikes people of all ages. “I did not know a thing about cardiac arrest. I didn’t know what it was,” Blake said. Now she knows firsthand about sudden cardiac arrest. The more she found out, the more she wondered why more people weren’t educated about SCA, the leading causes of death in the U.S. Just a few months after Blake’s sudden cardiac arrest

According to the World Bank, The US ranks 50th in maternal mortality globally - falling behind every other industrialised nation. A woman in the US is as likely to suffer a maternal death as a woman in sanctions-hit Iran, and four times as likely as a woman in Germany.
"It is a preventable health issue," said Amnesty International's Rachel Ward. "It isn't something that we're waiting for a cure for. We’re waiting for political will."
What's worse is that US maternal mortality rates may actually be on the rise. Eugene Declercq, of Boston University's School of Public Health, produced a short film named Birth by the numbers, discussing maternal mortality in the US. In a 2013 follow-up report, he noted how mortality rates in other industrialised nations had declined - but in the US, it increased by 30 percent in the first decade of the new millennium.

as little surprise. But it remains a shocking fact nonetheless.
The stark figures come from the 2018 Intensive Care National Survey (ICNSS). It is the fourth National Survey of Expectant and Newborn Women in America to show that the vast majority of these deaths could have been avoided.
Here is what the survey found.
Many women with US birth infections are simply not treated
The problems in America are widespread. About 10 out of every 1,000 pregnant women will develop an obstetric infection, most often strep throat. In half of these cases, vaginal candidiasis and urinary tract infections are the cause. In most cases, these are fatal. But, unfortunately, most mothers in America suffer no diagnosis until it is too late.
The ICNSS also explores why this happens. Most likely it is because of the lack of comprehensive and timely care.

How many American women die of pregnancy-related causes every day?
According to the American Cancer Society, nearly 41,500 women are diagnosed with breast cancer in America every year and 17,800 die from it. About 55,000 people die from colon cancer, and around 5,700 die from pancreatic cancer.
The most common cause of cancer death is lung cancer, lung disease, heart disease and early onset diabetes.
6 Five out of 10 deaths in America are caused by cigarette smoking, which kills more people in this country than it does elsewhere Credit: Getty - Contributor
This means that over 24,800 women and women and men in America die from an obstacle related to pregnancy per year.
The mortality rate from malaria in America, for example, is only slightly more than that from lung cancer.
The rate of cancer deaths in America is actually about 40% higher than the world average.

It’s not the food—the grub is OK, if not exactly slimming; it’s the gas generators powering the carts. Most food carts run off a diesel generator that’s designed to run only a few hours. Vendors run them for stretches of up to 14 hours, leading to a high output of greenhouse-gas emissions such as carbon monoxide, nitrous oxide, and particulate matter. You can see the smoke with the naked eye, but the hard facts are even more frightening: The research and consulting firm Energy Vision found that each cart produces the same amount of nitrous oxide as 186 cars on the road.
A new pilot program between the city and a Queens-based company called MOVE Systems aims to cut down on that pollution by at least 60 percent. For the past year, MOVE has been fine-tuning a new kind of food cart

Another passenger said that it looked like four shots had caused damage.

Baden-Württemberg police said officers were investigating for "dangerous interference with rail traffic" and have urged any witnesses to come forward.

screen villains, too, like Lex Luthor and Diana's father, Arthur Curry.

central Europe, and it was sometimes the capital of the Holy Roman Empire. Its ancient center was architecturally and many other ways romantic and gorgeous. Because it was subject only to rather minor bombing, Prague’s charm and beauty were nearly unscathed by World War II.
But 45 years of socialist “compassion” under a Soviet Union puppet regime had turned everything soot gray and ugly before eastern and central Europe was liberated in 1989.
When I arrived, you could walk down the winding cobblestone streets of the Old Town near its famous castle amid five-story ancient residential buildings standing shoulder-to-shoulder against narrow sidewalks on both sides. Buildings that had once been vibrant with color, exquisite in their features and soaring to the sky were now soot-gray dingy, decaying and seemingly drooping in shame.
Then, out of nowhere, came one that was a brilliant pastel lime-green, fully restored and bursting with light and life. Then so

Europe, encompassing the harsh medieval past with its bivouac underground, its medieval past with its crumbling gothic buildings in their famous Old Town and many wonderful surprises of its medieval past. It was a fascinating place to live for the law - graduates get many but crucially limited opportunities to practice law abroad, unlike, say, Russia or Spain, or the Philippines and many other parts of the world - always with a network to help you when the devil is in the details. It was very rewarding. Prague was, however, also extraordinarily lonely, especially after the Soviet Union began dismantling. I stayed only a very limited few days, but already I was full of nostalgia for past Prague, wishing I was there every day to help keep order. The last global climate summit held in Copenhagen, Denmark, in 2009 provided a clear route to take. But the ambition shown and the commitments made were too small and too insufficient for the "solution for us" approach. If governments were serious, every country in the world will have to play an effective part, otherwise, global warming is merely just a pipe dream.

The important question is where we go from here? The finance, research and development and innovation sectors have the capacity to move the pace. According to the Boston Consulting Group (BCG), these sectors can produce 5 to 10 times as much of the energy the world needs within the next 15 to 20 years. In addition to cutting costs and increasing availability, the innovations for the energy and infrastructure sectors have to be integrated into other market mechanisms for the long term. Currently there is only one model for this: production.

On May 3, a group of 12 ducklings went missing. The women noticed them struggling and realized their stomachs were badly matted with their blankets, which looked real rotten. They called Turlock Animal Control for help.
Officers believe it was someone who was running around loose in the middle of the afternoon.
“It does make you feel a little safer in our city,” said Dawn Harman of Turlock Animal Control. "That mean they want to play, they want to go for a walk and they get attracted to other animals."
The officers immediately noticed the "glaring white strips" on the ducklings' necks. Officer Steve Crabtree personally examined the animal and determined it was grossly malnourished.
Since the agency handles all domesticated animals, officers said it was imperative that the flock be humanely removed from Donnelly Park.
Officers are unsure if the same people are responsible.

The Generation Energy report was produced by an advisory committee appointed by Mr. Carr after he hosted a conference last year on the need for a new Canadian energy strategy. The committee included representatives from the oil and gas industry and the electricity sector, as well as from clean energy and Indigenous organizations.
The panel declared that climate change is a growing danger, and that Canada must be part of a global transition to lower-carbon energy needed to avert the most catastrophic impacts.

The big picture: The long-term targets laid out in the report, backed by a recent review commissioned by the minister, suggest Canada is poised to meet some of the goals set by the Paris agreement but fall well behind a promised increase in efforts to cut emissions from 2030 onwards. The study also included the inner-eye photos taken by the ER staff using an ocular camera.
Then the study team assessed the response of two ophthalmologists who viewed and rated the pictures on a desktop PC and did the same on an iPhone.
The results of the study, published in the Archives of Ophthalmology, show that the doctors found the iPhone images good or better than desktop images and rated them high.
While one ophthalmologist felt 53 pictures were of the same quality, he found 46 better on iPhone and just one was better on PC.
For the other ophthalmologist who participated in the study, 56 were of equal quality, while 42 were better on iPhone leaving only two better on PC.

To ease the patients, experts from Utsav Foundation for Women invited a group of Ophthamologists and patient groups to determine the health risk of smartphones among people. The study was carried out in Hyderabad.
The study findings were published in Human Protein Observatory. It found that doctors checking patients was less likely to use the smartphone, and that eyeglass users were more likely to look at the smartphone than the eye.
The researchers chose to ask participants about their current and past use of smartphone so that it was studied from an academic perspective, implying the significance of the data.
The study mentions that use of the iPhone was linked to eyeglass users, wearing sunglasses was linked to doctors looking at the screen while wearing an glasses, and using laptops were linked to using glasses.

Bells.
“Children laughing, people passing, meeting smile after smile.” I love how old movies portray New York City at Christmastime.
But I grew up in New York. And the city is nothing like that at Christmastime. The tree does light up Rockefeller Center and decorations line the streets. If you're lucky you'll get a nice snowfall. But what gives the city in those old films the spirit of Christmas is the people. The “children laughing” and the “smile after smile.” Ralph Kramden described it best in the Christmas episode of The Honeymooners, “everyone's hustling someplace. But they don't hustle around Christmastime like they usually do. You know they're a little more friendlier … they bump into you, they laugh and they say, 'Pardon me. Merry Christmas.'” It's a lovely idea but in reality New York is New York.

Bells Are Ringing. Without all of that, it wouldn't be Christmas. Thank goodness, I don't have to see it this year, but I remember the NYC Lights Parade and it being dark as the night we leave on holiday break from work. We'll have to make a little visit this Christmas Eve, even though we're having an early morning little Christmas dinner out in Oakland. We'd better hurry though, this might be the last Christmas my 6'3" husband and I have left together!

Movieclips Classic Classics – Holiday ReleasesThe first look at the pictures from Clark Gable's Oscar® winning Christmas movie, A Day at Christmas. Is there a more magical place on earth than the Christmas movie? Is there a more Christmassy object than the tree at Rockefeller Center? A Day at Christmas is one of my absolute all time favorite films, and I was thrilled when the Academy Award®-winning Clark Gable version re-opened in 1999 at the Biltmore on Central Park West in NYC. Perfect Christmas dinner at the Biltmore is always a must-do activity in NYC. I also love the Short Film, The Christmas Tree in Park Avenue with Bing Crosby and Charles Durning. Maybe most famous for the song Christmas Waltz, this movie is definitely one of the most underappreciated.

Christmas Train
The Logan County Library began its tradition of Christmas trains a few years ago to give people of all ages something to look forward to as the holiday season starts to take over. Those participating in the Christmas trains can expect a visit from Santa, along with a visit by a train operator that narrates and teaches children about the history of the Christmas trains. They can also stop at plenty of stops along the road to look at various historical sites or hear about the people involved in the history of the Christmas train.
The Logan County Christmas Train runs the tracks from November 24 to December 23 with an approximate stop every 90 minutes.
The schedule of the Christmas Train in 2021 can be found here: https://bit.ly/3oVo0wD
To view the route of the Christmas Train, check the railroad’s Facebook page ( https://bit.ly/3oR1r9H) for the next stop on the schedule.

Bruce Kaider have teamed up with Los Angeles producers Zachary Green and Jason Shuman to create a film about Delly’s life. It will focus on his upbringing in Australia and lead right up into the present day, which has taken Dellavedova to the magical city of...Milwaukee. No, but seriously, Kaider is promising that there will be plenty of intriguing stops along the way.

Dellavedova added that he’s looking forward to getting started on the film. "I am honored that Bruce, Zachary, and Jason think enough about my journey to turn it into a feature film," he said. Jason Rabil have been meeting with other studios since the summer to discuss the possibility of making the biopic. Yes, that’s the same Jason Rabil who once advised—well, “advised”—Brad Pitt on the project for “Moneyball.”
Of course, it’s not an officially announced biopic of Delly. Rather, it’s all speculation at this point. But Jason Rabil said he had not heard any objections from either Disney or Fox, making him hopeful.

11 such world-famous ideas from the history of science and technology. The temptation, of course, is to include those ideas that were scorned and should have stayed scorned. The Star Wars prequels, say. But we're trying to run a classy operation here.
Heliocentrism Galileo Galilei, sometimes called the Father of Modern Science, was among the first and most famous to pay a price for his crazy scientific ideas. Galileo publicly promoted the Copernican concept of heliocentrism -- that the Earth revolves around the Sun -- back when the Church and even most fellow mathematicians held to geocentric model. Unfortunately, in 17th century Rome, such radical ideas were quite literally heresy. Galileo was forced to recant and spend the rest of his life under house arrest.

t make him insufferable. His mind has trumped his batting feats, which are nondescript — 1,952 international runs at an average of 23.23. But he is extraordinarily cerebral, and it’s primarily due to this that he played 64 international matches, of which he captained in 56. His brain kept him in the team; if not captaining, he wouldn’t have been in the team for so long. His leadership qualities were exceptional, his man management top-drawer — post-cricket, he became a psychoanalyst.

t spare him from an unhappy life: his friend Chris Brogan, just after his dissertation on the same subject, consoled Brearley by inviting him on to an unsuccessful selection for the national side. Like a famous cricket journalist, he acquired a stodgy reputation, but then wrote for the wonderfully named Haywardshire Mercury, staying at the heart of the Durham action from the beginning. was really badly done," Gutmann, from the University of Auckland, New Zealand, said while giving a talk on Vista content protection. "Once the enemy is the user and not the attacker, standard security thinking falls apart."
Vista requires premium content like high-definition movies to be degraded in quality when sent to high-quality outputs, so users are seeing status codes that say "graphics OPM resolution too high." Gutmann calls this "probably the most bizarre status code ever."
While Microsoft's intent is to protect commercial content, home movies are increasingly being shot in high definition, Gutmann said. Many users are finding they can't play any content if it's considered "premium."
"This is not commercial HD content being blocked, this is the users' own content," Gutmann said. "The more premium content you have, the more output is disabled."

The encryption attacks primarily target, but are not limited to, information stored in DNS files and registry keys, Gutmann said. In addition, users have also seen DNS files infected with viruses known as MS13-067 in versions of Windows 8.

Columnists William Kristol and Charles Krauthammer grumble that the Administration is operating under too many constraints. Impatience with the air campaign has sparked calls by the Sunday morning punditocracy to send in substantial ground troops beyond the small contingent already there. Senator John McCain calls for B-52 carpet-bombing. Polls show a rising number of Americans doubt the war on terrorism will succeed.
Our concern is less about fine points of military strategy than about the possibility that the human and political costs of the war might outweigh any gains in national security and undermine America's moral credibility in the fight against the perpetrators of September 11. The bombing campaign may or may not be militarily effective–who knows, since our only information comes from the Pentagon and Al Jazeera–but civilian casualties are eroding support among coalition allies. TV pictures of devastated neighborhoods and wounded civilians fuel anger against America

a time to hunker down. For many, that means seeking pleasure in long, slow cooking.
In Manhattan, where I live, the idea of a hearth may be fanciful, but the warmth of the oven or stove, with a bubbling pot of beans or a simmering stew, serves the crucial figurative function.
The sorts of deep, soulful flavors that come from blends of herbs and spices, long braises, and meats and birds roasted on the bone require specific sorts of wines. Most often they are reds, but not just any sort of red. These foods demand staying power and the ability to stand up to robust flavors.
Yet they must also fulfill the fundamental roles of providing refreshment and enhancing meals without overpowering them. This means wines that are balanced and energetic, substantial rather than ethereal, yet not blusterous or domineering.

When you do science in a new way, at a new sensitivity, or under new, unique conditions, that's often where the most surprising, serendipitous discoveries are made. not be created by a single neutrino. An entire universe could be composed of neutrinos. But in astronomy the high success rate of neutrino astronomy means that there are good expectations for each results. You should use them, and check them later.
You might think that we're already on the road to neutrino astronomy. Something to point to and say "there's our neutrino," but the problem is you're looking in the wrong places. With high rates of neutrino astronomy, it's impossible to estimate the total number of neutrinos in a future star cluster or in the atmosphere, or whatever, of a bright star like the Sun.
Would we even notice the neutrinos? In astrophysics we're called instrument detectives, looking for anomalies, or clues, in the data. With high rates of neutrino astronomy, your margin for error is dramatically smaller.

second worst part of the MTV Movie Awards, however, is a little more difficult to pinpoint: The lengthy Verne Troyer sketch that was a commercial for Orbit gum? The decline is due to punishment through budget cuts by Congress for the agency’s egregious mismanagement and malpractice behaviors including Commissioner John Koskinen’s lying to Congress last year, which almost got him impeached, and the political profiling of Tea Party groups before that. The emphasis on audits by all of the popular tax preparers has changed since the Tax Reform Act of 1986 passed, making it difficult to identify which tax preparer(s) are actually hired by the IRS and auditing their work. But as I discussed last week in On-Time, most tax preparers are simply channelers who do not receive an additional fee for doing the initial preparation and filing of the tax return.

The IRS (as well as other agencies) will continue to audit those who do not file accurate tax statements, but I doubt they will audit those who do so only in good faith and include a Schedule C/HECM in their statements, because they want to ensure that those who do so do so in good faith and do so only to obtain information.

of a huge avocado tree. An apricot tree groaned with fruit during the summers.
I was married at the time, and my then-husband and I added a new garage to the property, painted the inside of the house, transformed the kitchen. I spent hours in the garden creating flower beds, planting new trees.
The places we live seem to absorb the memories we are making. We turn a corner and collide with the past. I got divorced while living in the house shaded by trees. Newly single, I struggled through a couple of other relationships, both of them turbulent and destructive. I also rescued a baby squirrel and nurtured him into adulthood, turning the treehouse into his home.
His name was Squirmy, and he and my dog, Sadie, chased each other around for hours, bound by a mysterious agreement that neither would ever catch up to the other.
Birds used to wait for me to put food on the bird feeder;

. Beds were everywhere. Perhaps I have never known better. I met people that cared. I sang in the church choir, played the guitar, drum and fiddle. I even got some concert performance scholarships for the fiddle. Yes, my life had its ups and downs. But I loved it. I loved the shelter.
As I stepped through the front door to discover another day, I was feeling great and a part of life. I wondered what the next thing was. It was a shock to come down on the second floor and find myself in bed alone, scared of what the day held. I was bound for the Valley Inn Motel in Blackstone, a must on the tourist map, known for its biker bars. I woke up late that morning, tired of the sun beating down on the couch; a motel was a dream come true. I stepped out onto the porch, opened the screen door to let in the fresh air and closed it, it was already night

are wrestling with questions of justice, writing at the intersection of history and intimacy, weaving political and social truth-telling into their private experiences.
Robert Pinsky defended his art while serving as poet laureate: “I think poetry is a vital part of our intelligence, our ability to learn, our ability to remember, the relationship between our bodies and minds,” he told the Christian Science Monitor. Bay Area poets Solmaz Sharif and Tess Taylor prove this claim, help us learn and remember with their vital new collections.
Sharif’s astonishing debut Look (Graywolf Press; $16) examines an endless cycle of war and violence, using a myriad of voices and forms. A formidable poetic talent, Sharif was born in Istanbul to Iranian parents, activists who fled their home country after the Iranian Revolution.

reject the social and political fictions of the “our songs” era and prefer to put their songs to the test of suffering and rights violations experienced by ordinary people.

One recent illustration: before World War Two, Chinese-language poetry (which is hardly poetry) usually addressed ordinary people’s needs—child care and public transport among other things. But as Mao Zedong began to take control, poets began to examine and critique the inner nature of Chinese politics and the widening gap between the ruled and ruled-for-all classes. In his early books, Mao ultimately became the primary poet to ask about the meaning behind Song of the Troubled Waters, a poem published in 1931 and composed in self-effacement (responding to a request from his buddies), drawing heavily on the confusion of the Sino-Japanese War (1931).

The adage "art or war" has survived a long and difficult contest of practice against society's will, but in today's world, writing about war and suffering becomes increasingly difficult and demand is ever higher. I put the task aside on the easy days, when the backbreaking work isn't too bad and I have long days to devote to lolling about at the library or taking naps at farm camps. But whenever I look out onto a sunny day, it's harder for me to imagine that for Central New Yorkers the tragedy that was I-81 runs without brakes.

Shasta Creek Apartments east of Interstate 5.
The work is visible from off north Hilltop Drive.
Palomar Builders is grading land to build attached homes, much like its Park Pointe neighborhood just north of this site.
But this project has a twist.
Jeb Allen, Palomar’s co-owner, told me about 10 of the 46 units will have studio apartments where the garage would go. The apartments will have a bathroom and kitchen.
Allen had tried to sell the property. But it's zoned multi-family, so Allen said nobody would buy it because the cost to build apartments didn't pencil out.
So, he came up with the studio apartment idea, which satisfies the requirement to build multi-family housing.
“When I start something, I want to finish it, and to me this finishes Highland Park,” Allen said.
Highland Park is the single-family subdivision next to Park Pointe

Mission De Oro Library, at 5701 Hilltop Drive. Oh, also: The one-acre area from Paisano Drive to Sidney Embarrass Road also saw a parcel there being prepared for a big foundation, according to photos that circulated on Twitter.
The work came to a halt when Mission San Juan School District officials left this morning.
The only explanation for this sound disturbing my senses is they are building the foundations for a civic facility. Area is filled with broken down tools. Look at the big brake drums on the "boathouse." Clearly dirt. Let's show they were cleared and marked right so that a civic function is allowed. Palomar Medical Center on the corner of Hilltop Drive and Mission De Oro Drive.
Call it the Mission De Oro Hilltop, or Mission De Oro Hilltop Heights, it doesn't matter much. That's because Palomar health officials recently came under fire from neighbors who live nearby over plans to clear the area for a future development that would include a hotel.
The proposed 24-room car service hotel will be located between Mission De Oro Drive on the west and Hilltop Drive on the east. But it wasn't always a part of the county's vision.
Back in 2006, work on the project began, but ground soon gave out and that's when the locals complained, county officials say. Then in 2010, officials started making changes that left many of the homes and businesses with the new structure.
"They needed to put in better foundations. They had lots of utilities, but none were underground," said Robin Granados, a neighbor on Mission De Oro Drive.

On Hollywood Boulevard, just past the man with the albino python wrapped around his neck, lies the TCL Chinese Theatre. Four years ago, the iconic landmark received a multimillion dollar makeover to revive its faded beauty, not unlike some of the aging actors who’ve placed their hands and feet in the theater’s cement courtyard.
But Thursday , the majestic movie theater will mark 90 years as the Grand Dame of Hollywood Boulevard, playing host to 20,000 visitors a day from around the globe.
“It’s just like it looks on TV,” marveled Jay and Nazira Momin, who were visiting from Atlanta.
These days it hosts more than 40 movie premieres a year, and it screens the blockbusters moviegoers can’t wait to see each weekend.

January 23, 2018 (Spring Valley) - ECM traveled to Granite Hills on Tuesday night for a clash between the lady Eagles and the lady Wolf Pack on the court. Granite Hills has started their season a little lackluster, going 7-10 overall and 1-1 in league. The Wolf Pack sport the same 7-10 record, but are undefeated so far in league, going 2-0.
The Wolf Pack set the tone in the first quarter, but were held back in the second by a hungry Eagles offense who crept back into the game. West Hills managed to keep their lead going into the half by eight points, 18-10.
Not very many points were scored in the third, but West Hills senior Kerrie Daniels got the Pack to a double digit lead with a nice putback on the offensive board and the Pack led 23-13 after three.

Whenever a stock or market gets highlighted, it’s generally very close to a turn and usually close to high or low; in this case I can make a solid claim that there are enough gyrations to suggest the advance from the April 2018 lows has ended an impulse wave (5 wave rally).
To support the more bearish idea (Idea 1) and a large move lower to at least correct the advance from the April 2018 lows, then I would want to see a move below 24.90. The alternative idea that I am tracking (Idea 2), suggests the recent high is only that of a 3rd of 3rd wave and a few more gyrations as shown would be seen to extend the advance.
However if we see a big move below 24.90, then I feel the depth of the decline would not be fitting with the prior gyrations and is likely part of a move lower for Idea 1.

Mark tried to ignore a recent cancer scare. He took walks and still would go to work. But the warmth of the sun on his face shimmered with the reminder of his poor diagnosis. My guess is that this crackdown is hardly a huge program, so it’s not as if loads of resources are being diverted to make life more difficult for the poor. Beyond that, though, Obama seems to instinctively get something that the rest of us lefties probably ought to appreciate more: like it or not, if you want the public to support government programs, you need to make sure they’re administered effectively. That’s doubly or triply true of social welfare programs, which are easily demagogued even in the best of times. If anything, liberals who support these programs ought to be more concerned about rooting out fraud and improving efficiency than conservatives, who’d be just as happy to see them simply go away.
This is fundamentally a Charlie Peters-ish neoliberal insight, and neoliberalism has obviously taken a lot of lumps over the past decade. Some of them were deserved, some weren’t. Either way

Being an exceptionally productive writer, he composed commentaries on Aristotle, Euclid and Plato, systematic treatises in all disciplines of philosophy as it was at that time (metaphysics and theology, physics, astronomy, mathematics, ethics) and exegetical works on traditions of religious wisdom (Orphism and Chaldaean Oracles). Proclus had a lasting influence on the development of the late Neoplatonic schools not only in Athens, but also in Alexandria, where his student Ammonius became the head of the school. In a culture dominated by Christianity, the Neoplatonic philosophers had to defend the superiority of the Hellenic traditions of wisdom. Continuing a movement that was inaugurated by Iamblichus (4th c.) and the charismatic figure of emperor Julian, and following the teaching of Syrianus, Proclus was eager to demonstrate the harmony of the ancient religious revelations (the

) of the school of Aristotle. With the growth of his scientific and historical results, several other avenues than direct imitation were sought, which included abandoning his implied dialogues, rendering more explicitly metaphorical and figurative, and using his methodical reasoning in constructing explicit metaphysical claims (i.e. abstract philosophical principles). However, most new philosophers of the end of the fourth century either became experts on the methodology of Aristotle or else tried to do so. Euthyphro (*468–470 C.E.) was one of the few philosophers of the fourth century who did not seek the substitution of metaphysics for logic, at least in his philosophical works. Euthyphro acquired a great theoretical reputation. In his book ‘Letter to Peeur’s Evoluciion’, Euthyphro also founded a systematic way of analysing and defining abstract ideas. Euthyphro’s most famous work (first edited from a lost editorial copy)

Nazarbayev left with his wife on Monday, opting to hand over the reins of the Central Asian country after a fresh round of street protests demanding better civil rights and sweeping economic reforms.
The 86-year-old Nazarbayev has waged an authoritarian political battle since the death in 2017 of his father who led Kazakhstan for decades and for most of the 1990s.
The resignation comes amid fears over allegations of crimes against humanity linked to repression during Kazakhstan's brutal civil war decades ago -- including the killing of some 8,000 demonstrators.
The announcement didn't make an official appointment, but President Askar Mamin declared Nazarbayev a member of the National Security Council -- one of the five key posts in his administration.

the country's long-time ruler and in his first official act renamed the capital after his predecessor.
Kassym-Jomart Tokayev took office in a pomp-filled ceremony less than 24 hours after Nursultan Nazarbayev, the only leader an independent Kazakhstan had ever known, suddenly announced he was stepping down.
Tokayev immediately proposed changing the name of the Central Asian nation's capital from Astana to Nursultan, or "Sultan of Light" in Kazakh, and parliament approved the change within hours.
The senate also appointed Nazarbayev's eldest daughter Dariga Nazarbayeva as speaker, setting her up as a potential contender to succeed her father.
Tokayev, 65, will serve out the rest of Nazarbayev's mandate until elections due in April next year, though the former president retains significant powers in the country he ruled for nearly three decades.

The land he’s farming has been in the family since the early 1800s.
Now a firefighter in Loganville, Moon has 13 more years until retirement and is planning to keep farming right up until retirement and beyond. He hopes to pass along at least some of the land to his children.

One way to solve this problem is to bypass the credit issue entirely. Many privately-owned homes and apartment buildings do not look at credit, preferring to find tenants with good references from previous landlords. You can find advertisements for privately-owned rentals in the classified section of your newspaper, on the internet at sites like Craig's List and Rental.com, as well as through local real estate agents.
Many local homeowners and condo owners rent their houses because they are having trouble selling them in the current real estate market, so you may be able to find a very nice property at a reasonable rate, provided you are a good tenant and are willing to keep the place clean and in order.

is asking for approval to provide free preschool to all 4-year-olds — a noble idea. Because of how the author and co-authors have chosen to fund the bill, it places these 4-year-olds in our public school system. I would imagine any person in the field of early childhood development; teacher, psychologist and doctor would oppose this bill.
The bill states that through longitudinal studies it has been found that those children who attended quality preschool programs had higher graduation rates, college enrollment rates and earning rates. This is what preschool educators have provided for years. Four-year-olds still need help developing social skills and gaining confidence. They need space inside and out allowing them to explore their environment. This will not happen at an elementary school. Elementary schools predominantly have blacktop as the surface outside. Inside the classroom, the environment will not be warm and inviting as in a quality preschool and won’t have the supplies needed to create and invent.
What about working parents that

, D-Sacramento and Senator Bill Dodd, D-Napa, is changing kindergarten instruction to facilitate 30 minutes of special education time per day to children from Pre-K to 3rd grade, beginning on the fourth day of school in 2014. Presently this is an option for students who live within five miles of a school with an approved special education service (SES) plan. Children eligible for the tax benefits associated with being a part of a school that services children with SES are not required to use these 30 minutes of special education time. Instead, families, educators and those who are philanthropy minded want children in California’s pre-K to go at least every 90 minutes of special education instruction. Other kindergarten specialists and unions in California are challenging Steinberg’s effort, and it will take a federal court ruling to settle this debate. ographer: Glen Ligon
Glenn Ligon, the artist behind palindromes and palindromic poems, turned 90 this year. His last public, visible outing for his art was in 2014, but his questions haven’t gone away: What are palindromes, or odd sentences that, ironically enough, lack direction? What happens to my body when I die? Is there any afterlife? Is God a real thing? Who's the world's greatest poet? What’s the difference between science and religion? These, and many more questions, have prompted Ligon to collect, annotate, and translate notable palindromes in his new book of essays, A Palindrome Without Words: Palindromes Without a Human Value, produced and published by Prometheus Books.
Longtime Ligon collaborator James Wagner specializes in art history and literary criticism. Together, they have also explored the appropriation of other forms of nonlinear culture: palindromes

In the other, it’s a sentence in which the phrase you’re using is in reverse order, but isn’t what you’re thinking about at all. In either form, the phrase you’re using is something very confusing and obnoxious, so that you’re tempted to think of the phrase the other way around. If you feel more connected naturally, you perform better,” said Alan Turner, principal of the Mountain View architectural firm Hawley, Peterson and Snyder, which designed the Hewlett Foundation’s Menlo Park headquarters, one of California’s first buildings to earn a gold rating from the U.S. Green Building Council.

Could Trump’s EPA actually be doing something helpful for our planet and health for a change? Maybe! But let’s not get too ahead of ourselves here.
The proposed “Cleaner Truck Initiative” seeks to decrease nitrogen oxide (NOx) emissions from heavy-duty trucks, which can worsen or help spur asthma and other respiratory issues when inhaled regularly. The trial starts nine months after Malaysians voted Najib out of office in a general election dominated by public disgust over allegations some US$4.5 billion (S$6.1 billion) was stolen from 1MDB, and about a quarter of it went into his personal bank accounts.
Police found nearly US$300 million worth of goods and cash at properties linked to Najib soon after the May 2018 election.
The 65-year-old son of Malaysia's second prime minister is also trying to build an image as a folksy voice of working people, especially members of the ethnic Malay majority.
Najib, in a viral video last month, crooned a Malay-language version of the 1970s hit Kiss And Say Goodbye, surrounded by a chorus of sombre young singers, criticising Mahathir's coalition for failing to live up to election promises.

Najib, 66, made an emotional return to the news headlines on Friday, appearing before reporters at Pakatan Harapan (PH) headquarters in Kuala Lumpur.
The widely-expected return came just two days after a scandal over real estate deals involving one of his companies reached a jaw-dropping legal conclusion.
His new trial - his sixth since a shock election victory in 2009 - is scheduled to start on Monday with massive public interest due to allegations he profited from billions of dollars of state and corporate funds.
All of the charges relate to SRC International, a trading firm that was set up in the late 1990s and used to recruit top Malaysian officials, and 1MDB - the energy firm often touted as a pet project of Najib's that ended in collapse in 2014.
On Friday, Najib was forced to defend the "stability" of the country's multi-billion-dollar economic development.

There's no need to go bald any more.
Baldness is a complex polygenic trait: up to five genes are involved, and it is the interplay between these genes, not unlike the interaction between the cards in a poker hand, that determine the specifics of male pattern hair loss.
So, what are the treatment options?
Finasteride is a Therapeutic Goods Administration-approved drug that dermatologists and general practitioners have been prescribing to treat hair loss for about 15 years.
It works by stopping the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone in the prostate, by blocking an enzyme called 5-alpha reductase.
You'll need a prescription for finasteride, and your doctor will explain the benefits and risks of the drug before you decide to proceed.
The benefits are clear: men who start taking daily finasteride at the first signs of hair loss will not go bald.

Surgical treatment for hair loss is fairly consistent with the treatment for hair growth and generally the same. Metaphysics special: What is consciousness?
We do know that any sense we have of being in control of our actions is, to some extent, an illusion. In particular, neuroscientist Benjamin Libet showed in the 1980s that mechanisms within the brain initiate actions long before that brain’s owner is aware of deciding to perform them.
It’s a big extrapolation to claim that all of our actions are outside our control. “Libet deals with the very short-term precursors of very simple actions,” says Patrick Haggard,

I believe in "the slippery slope": When you do enough mental gymnastics to discover the meaning of the things you experience, your (mis-)interpretations will lead you to discover the mystery of metaphysics.

God or a decision-maker outside of us – decides what we will and will not do.
This dilemma, philosophers and theologians have been wrestling with since the dawn of reason. We might use the analogy of the Australian Football League. Suppose there is a live football game and a final score – it’s a big game, but a fairly boring one – but also a final conversion: that is, no other team scores after three quarters of football and all the goals get stopped immediately because the kicking area is reduced to 15 meters or 15 meters squared.
The late Brian Hartley discovered one day this was the end of the game. I wondered, since a football game had gone on longer than three quarters of football – it’s a very long game, the final score – what decided this final conversion? So, a colleague and I went looking for the answer in the laws of gravity – a very long and complex engine that controlled one of the fundamental particles of the universe.

A novice election employee inserted a card from a vote machine to tally the results on it. But the election employee ejected the card before the machine counted its votes, erasing all of the information on the card, Tippecanoe County Clerk Christa Coffey said about 12:15 a.m. Wednesday.
Meanwhile, crowds gathered and waited for election returns in the Tippecanoe Room at the County Building, frequently asking why it was taking so long to count the votes.
As Coffey talked with reporters about the mistake, it had been barely 45 minutes since the final results were published on a very late election night that followed a very long Election Day.
Fortunately, the card did not erase all of its information.
Election workers matched the card to a specific machine from which it was pulled, Coffey said. Then they located where in Tippecanoe County that machine was.

The word apparently had legs as President Donald Trump spewed it about 270 Electoral College votes and yet-to-be-ascertained victory, suggesting the U.S. Postal Service might not mail ballots in time.
Few, of course, believe him: 10 days, 362 counties, 5 million mail-in ballots, 41 states planning for the Postal Service to operate as they normally would on Election Day.
But had the Postal Service been slow to send millions of mail-in ballots, it raises the same questions that change in administration raises: Which governments should be trusted to administer elections? Which people?
Another question: Will mail-in ballots arrive in all the counties in time to vote in enough time? Will people really understand their voting rights? Will states have the necessary bureaucracy in place to facilitate participation? Will absentee voters face new difficulties with their ballots on Election Day?

I'm pretty sure this bird is a female based on the color of her feet, and the color of her cere (the "bump" above her beak). I understand that Parakeets can live 20 years, so acquiring a bird, like any pet, should not be taken lightly.
Personally, I love having a bird around. I've had a rescued Cockatiel for many years and she alerts me to someone coming to the door - before my dogs! They are intelligent, interactive and fun.
Feel free to read up on Parakeet care before taking the plunge. Here are a couple articles that I found interesting.
If you'd like to meet and possibly adopt Jake, please contact me directly at sheri@petraits.com or 773-777-2891. Her adoption fee is $25, or $50 if you'd also like her cage, food, toys and treats. She's being fostered in Chicago's Andersonville neighborhood.

She can be heard saying "Whoa!" in other bird speak and still saying hey when you call. In addition to talking, she can learn different tricks and actions that the kids learned to play with her.
The mother who was fostering Jake told us she and her husband wanted to adopt another parakeet, but when they found hers for sale they asked for it to go back. Since then they have continued to think about giving Jake to the right family. If you are a good candidate and interested, come by the Farmers Branch Public Library, at 23300 Highway 249 N., from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. on Friday, March 23 to meet her. (For pets meet the pet list on the library's website.)
Leave your name and phone number in case you hear her call. Also, come ready to be pestered with bird-talk. But don't worry, when you do hand over your name, she is the sweetest kitty ever

Gov. Greg Abbott refused to pick sides Wednesday in a growing legislative squabble over how best to cut state taxes, and moved away from an earlier promise to “insist” that Texas lawmakers cut property taxes before the session ends on June 1.
At a press conference coinciding with the dreaded April 15 income tax filing deadline, Abbott hailed the Legislature’s heated debate over taxes as a sign of fiscal health when other states are struggling financially.
Abbott said he still feels property taxes are too high and noted that cutting them is “definitely on the table.” He was referring to the $2.15 billion property tax proposal being pushed by Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick, the Tea Party Republican who presides over the Senate.
In a statement Wednesday afternoon, Patrick repeated his support for the property tax cut.

has acted as a buffer to slow down the greenhouse gas accumulation in the atmosphere and, thus, global warming. However, this process also increases the acidity of seawater and can affect the health of marine organisms and the ocean ecosystem.
New research by University of Delaware oceanographer Wei-Jun Cai and colleagues at Université Libre de Bruxelles, Texas A&M University-Corpus Christi, University of Hawaii at Manoa and ETH Zurich, now reveals that the water over the continental shelves is shouldering a larger portion of the load, taking up more and more of this atmospheric carbon dioxide.
The study findings, published in Nature Communications on Wednesday, Jan. 31, may have important implications for scientists focused on understanding the global carbon budget.
Understanding how carbon flows between land, air and water is key to predicting how much greenhouse gas emissions the earth, atmosphere and ocean can tolerate over a given time period to keep global warming and climate change at thresholds considered tolerable.

is carbon sinks, too, storing the carbon molecules they ingest up in its many deep-sea caverns, dank valleys, dense mushrooms called kelp forests and below the oceans' waves. But, much to our surprise, living organisms in this system also generate and store their own carbon, much of it volcanic, making these diverse and interdependent systems—dominated by different species, geography, time of life and circumstance—phenomenological holy grails.
This perspective, known as a “carbon cycle adaptation,” answers our most pressing questions.
Here we review what scientists have learned about this complex and interdependent phenomenon, whose signature—a fairly constant shift over time from deeply oceanic and highly carbon rich to deeply oceanic and highly carbon deficient—has been documented and reproduced thousands of times.
This is the first study of the ocean carbon cycle and limited to just the world’s coral reefs. But as bacteria and corals are widely used for carbon bank studies

He said it was “difficult” to see how the UK could revert its decision to leave the EU, taken last year through an in/out referendum, with prime minister Theresa May at the helm.
“It's a theoretical possibility, it's an option. All can happen in life,” said the Italian MEP, who noted that the UK's change of heart should come “through a democratic process”.
“You don't forget. Our position was not for Brexit,” he said.

Pittella, who sits on the main political grouping in the EU legislature alongside the pro-Brexit ERG political group.

Remembrance Sunday, which will begin the annual service to be held at Christ Church Cathedral commencing at 09:30 am and led by Reverend Canon Paul Kerr and other local ministers.
His Excellency the Governor, Commander British Forces, Members of the Legislative Assembly, visiting and resident South Atlantic veterans, together with Senior Officers of the Armed Services will be attending this Service. The Armoured Corps & Prince of Wales Division Band will provide musical accompaniment. During the Service a collection will be made for the Poppy Appeal.
The congregation is requested to be seated in the Cathedral by 9.15 am. Seating will be on a first come first served basis.
The Guards procession, Associations and SAMA Veterans will march to the Cross of Sacrifice after the service with accompaniment provided by elements of the Band. The flow of traffic will be controlled on Ross Road, Crozier Place and Ross Road East to permit this march. Youth organisations will be present at the Cross of Sacrifice.

at Missouri University of Science and Technology under engineering professor Reza Zoughi have developed a patented device that can show the inner structures of objects in real time by using millimeter and microwave signals.
Potential applications include the detection of cancerous skin cells, termite damage to buildings, or concealed weapons at secure zones like airports.
The tech could also be used for finding "defects in thermal insulating materials that are found in spacecraft heat insulating foam and tiles, space habitat structures, aircraft radomes and composite-strengthened concrete bridge members," Zoughi was quoted as saying in a release.
The prototype camera has been in development for several years--check out the vid below, from 2009.
In its current form, objects have to be placed between a transmitter for the microwave radiation and a collector. It can run for several hours on a laptop-size battery.
The researchers are planning to upgrade it to a single unit that works more like a

at Carnegie Mellon University have proposed an experimental wrist computer which takes X-rays of the hand and strain-gages them against microcontact cases placed against objects to form a 3D model of the object's material composition. That information can then be visualized in 3D on the screen of the computer.
"This use of the sensor for physical microcontact imaging ... has not really been applied outside of academic research," researchers wrote. "A x-ray sensor device that observes subatomic particles interacting with whatever the end of the antenna is attached to – and that is carried like an invisibility cloak, suspended in air or suspended on the wrist – could be a highly relevant tool in sensory equipment."
The researchers are aiming for their wrist computer project, known as Z-PAT (z-printer forznarry), to be released in 2022. Z-PAT will use the same concept as X-ray glasses – the idea being to image material objects by using waves from tiny antennas

There is little doubt Britain will exit the EU in two years' time. But May's efforts to shepherd a deal through her cabinet will be fraught with trouble because other European allies oppose the negotiating aims she set out in her statement on Friday, and will be reluctant to let her get away without some EU concessions.
The most contentious section calls for the future of the EU's single market -- a proposal that Brussels will not tolerate. The "backstop" would keep Northern Ireland in the EU for some time after Brexit if that was how May could get the deal she was seeking. The backstop would also continue to cover the Irish border for some time after Brexit.
The British parliament also must approve May's Brexit deal, though whether it has any chance of passing this way has become less clear.
